pattern: DP where dp[i][j] records the side length of the largest all-ones square whose bottom-right corner is (i,j); each cell contributes its dp value to the total count because a square of side k contains exactly one bottom-right corner at every size 1..k.
hints: For every cell that is 1, ask: what is the largest all-ones square that ends here at the bottom-right? The answer depends on the three neighbours that could extend a square: the cell directly above, directly left, and diagonally up-left.
dp[i][j] = min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]) + 1 when matrix[i][j] is 1, because all three neighbours must have at least that side length for a larger square to be valid here.
A cell with dp value k contributes exactly k to the answer — it is the bottom-right corner of squares of side 1, 2, …, k — so a single pass summing all dp values gives the total count.

constraints.note: m rows, n cols, each dimension in 1..300

edge_inputs.description: entire matrix is zeros — no squares at all
entire matrix is ones — every sub-square of every size counts
matrix from first example — non-square region with partial all-ones blocks
matrix from second example — scattered ones break potential large squares
single row of all ones — only 1x1 squares possible
single column of all ones — only 1x1 squares possible
checkerboard pattern — no 2x2 or larger all-ones square exists

stress.description: large all-ones matrix — maximum square nesting, stresses the dp accumulation
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 300))
    matrix = [[1] * n for _ in range(n)]
    return [matrix]

stress.description: random 300x300 matrix with roughly half ones
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 300))
    matrix = [[rng.randint(0, 1) for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(n)]
    return [matrix]
